transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Alex. I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Uh, I don’t know. I guess, um, I’ve been thinking a lot about family stuff lately. Like, my cousin’s wedding is coming up, and I just… I don’t know. It feels like a lot. People will be there, and they’ll probably want to talk to me, and I just don’t really want to deal with that. It’s just… stressful, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like the upcoming wedding is weighing on you, especially with the thought of interacting with family. It’s understandable to feel stressed about that. Can you share a bit more about what specifically feels stressful for you in those interactions?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, I mean, it’s just that, um, I know they’ll ask questions. Like, what am I doing with my life, or why I’m not, you know, more involved or whatever. And I just don’t have good answers for that. I feel like they’ll judge me, and honestly, I don’t think they really want me there anyway. It’s just a lot of pressure, and I’d rather avoid it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're feeling a lot of pressure from the expectations and questions that might come from your family. It’s tough when you feel like you don’t have the answers to share, and that fear of judgment can be really heavy. When you think about those interactions, what emotions come up for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, I guess it makes me feel anxious, like really tense. I mean, I worry a lot about what they’ll think. It’s like, I don’t want to feel judged or, you know, like I’m disappointing anyone. It’s just… sad, I guess. I feel kind of lonely thinking about it all. I don’t know if that makes sense, but it’s just hard to shake off those feelings.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That makes a lot of sense, Alex. Feeling anxious and tense in those situations, especially with the fear of judgment and disappointment, can be really isolating. It’s understandable to feel sad and lonely when you’re anticipating those kinds of interactions. When you think about feeling lonely in this context, what do you think contributes to that feeling for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess it’s just that, um, I don’t really feel connected to them. Like, I don’t know if they really care about me or if they just see me as, I don’t know, someone who doesn’t fit in or whatever. It’s like, I’m there but not really there, if that makes sense? And then I think about how my mum is always critical, and that just adds to it. I don’t know, it’s like I’m just kind of floating around, and it feels lonely.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re feeling a deep sense of disconnection from your family, which can be really painful. The feeling of “floating around” and not truly being seen or valued can amplify that loneliness. It also seems like your mother’s critical nature adds to that pressure, making it harder to feel secure in those relationships. How do you usually cope with those feelings of disconnection and loneliness when they come up?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I don’t really know if I have good ways to cope, honestly. I guess I just try to avoid situations like this, like the wedding. I mean, ignoring the invitation felt easier than dealing with all that. I just kind of shut down, you know? It’s like, if I don’t engage, then I don’t have to face all those questions and judgments. But then, I guess it just leaves me feeling more alone afterward. It’s a bit of a cycle, I suppose.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like avoiding situations feels like a way to protect yourself from the anxiety and judgment, but it also leads to that cycle of loneliness afterward. Shutting down can seem like a safe option in the moment, but it leaves you feeling more isolated in the long run. It’s understandable to want to escape those uncomfortable feelings. When you think about this cycle, what do you wish could be different for you in those moments?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess I wish I could feel more comfortable, like, just being myself around them. It would be nice if I didn’t feel so judged or, I don’t know, like I had to explain myself all the time. Maybe if I could just be accepted for who I am, it wouldn’t feel so heavy. I mean, I know I can’t change how they are, but it would be nice if I could find a way to feel more at ease, you know? Just to not feel like I have to hide or avoid everything.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like a deep desire for acceptance and the freedom to be yourself without the weight of judgment. Feeling at ease in those situations would certainly lighten the emotional load you carry. It’s tough to navigate family dynamics when you sense that acceptance is lacking. What do you think might help you start to feel more comfortable being yourself around them, even if it’s just a little bit?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I don’t know. Maybe if I could find a way to, like, prepare for those conversations or something? Like, if I had some responses ready for their questions, it might help me feel less anxious. I guess I could also try to remind myself that their opinions don’t define me, but that’s easier said than done. I just… I wish I could find a way to feel more confident or something, but it’s hard to see how that could happen.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Preparing responses sounds like a practical step that could help you feel more in control during those conversations. It’s understandable to want to feel confident and to remind yourself that their opinions don’t define you, even if it feels challenging. It’s a process, and it’s okay to take small steps toward that confidence. What do you think might be the first small step you could take to start feeling a bit more prepared or confident for the wedding?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I suppose I could, um, think about some of the questions they might ask and come up with some answers ahead of time. Like, maybe I could practice saying them out loud or something. It feels a bit silly, but it might help me feel less caught off guard. I could also try to focus on, I don’t know, the positive aspects of being there, like seeing family I haven’t seen in a while. Just trying to shift my mindset a bit, I guess. It’s a start, at least.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a thoughtful approach, Alex. Preparing answers and practicing them can definitely help you feel less caught off guard, and focusing on the positive aspects of reconnecting with family is a great way to shift your mindset. It’s not silly at all; it’s a proactive step toward feeling more comfortable. As you think about these strategies, how do you feel about taking that first step?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I mean, it feels a bit daunting, to be honest. Like, I know I should do it, but part of me is still hesitant. I guess I worry that even with preparation, it might not go well, and I’ll still feel that pressure. But at the same time, I think it could help, so maybe I’ll give it a shot. It’s just… a little scary to think about putting myself out there like that. But I guess it’s better than just avoiding it completely, right?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely understandable to feel daunted by the idea of putting yourself out there, especially when you’re concerned about how it might go. Acknowledging that fear is an important part of the process. It sounds like you’re weighing the potential benefits of trying against the discomfort of facing those interactions. Taking that step, even if it feels scary, can be a brave move toward breaking the cycle of avoidance. How do you think you might support yourself as you take this step?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I guess I could remind myself that it’s okay to feel anxious and that it’s a normal part of the process. Maybe I could talk to someone I trust about it, just to get some support or encouragement. I could also try to focus on the fact that it’s just one day, and I don’t have to have it all figured out. Just taking it one step at a time might help. I don’t know, it’s a bit of a balancing act, but I think I could manage it if I keep reminding myself to be patient with myself.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those sound like really supportive strategies, Alex. Reminding yourself that it’s okay to feel anxious and seeking support from someone you trust can make a big difference. Focusing on the fact that it’s just one day and taking it one step at a time is a healthy perspective. It’s great that you’re recognizing the importance of being patient with yourself throughout this process. As you prepare for the wedding, what do you think would be the most helpful reminder for you to hold onto?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think, um, maybe reminding myself that I’m not alone in feeling this way could help. Like, a lot of people probably feel anxious in family situations, even if it doesn’t seem like it. And that it’s okay to just be myself, even if I’m not perfect or have everything figured out. Just trying to focus on being present and not getting too caught up in what others might think. I guess that could help me stay grounded, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That’s a wonderful reminder, Alex. Recognizing that many people share similar feelings can help you feel less isolated in your experience. Focusing on being present and accepting yourself as you are, imperfections and all, is a powerful way to stay grounded. It sounds like you’re developing a compassionate approach to this situation. As you move forward, how do you feel about checking in with yourself during the wedding to see how you’re doing?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think that could be really helpful. Just taking a moment to check in with myself, like, how I’m feeling in the moment, could help me stay aware of my emotions. I could remind myself that it’s okay to step away if I need a break or just take a breath. It might help me not get overwhelmed. I guess it’s about finding that balance between engaging and taking care of myself, right? So, yeah, I think checking in sounds like a good idea.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a very wise approach, Alex. Taking those moments to check in with yourself and giving yourself permission to step away if needed can help you maintain that balance between engaging with family and caring for your own well-being. It’s all about listening to your needs in the moment. As you prepare for the wedding, is there anything else you’d like to explore or discuss that might help you feel more ready?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I think I’ve covered a lot of what’s been on my mind. Just, you know, thinking through the preparation and how to handle things is helpful. I guess I just want to make sure I don’t get too caught up in my head when the day comes. Maybe just reminding myself to take it one step at a time and not overthink everything. I appreciate talking this through; it’s nice to have a bit of clarity.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m really glad to hear that talking it through has brought you some clarity, Alex. Taking it one step at a time and being mindful of not overthinking can definitely help you stay grounded on the day. It’s great that you’re finding ways to prepare and support yourself. If you ever want to revisit these thoughts or explore anything else in the future, I’m here for you. Is there anything else you’d like to share before we wrap up today?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’m good for now. Just, um, grateful for the chance to talk about this. It feels a bit lighter to have some strategies in mind. I’ll try to keep those reminders close as the wedding gets closer. Thanks for listening and helping me sort through all this. It means a lot.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Alex. I’m glad you found our conversation helpful and that it feels a bit lighter for you. It’s great that you have some strategies and reminders to hold onto as the wedding approaches. Remember, it’s okay to take things at your own pace. If you ever want to talk again or explore anything else, I’m here for you. Take care of yourself, and good luck with everything!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you, I appreciate that. I’ll definitely keep that in mind. It feels nice to have a bit of support. I’ll take care of myself, and I’ll try to remember everything we talked about. Thanks again for your help.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Alex. I’m glad to hear that you feel supported, and I believe you’ll do great as you navigate the wedding and your feelings. Remember to be gentle with yourself along the way. If you ever want to chat again, don’t hesitate to reach out. Wishing you all the best!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thanks, that really means a lot. I’ll try to be gentle with myself and keep those reminders in mind. I appreciate your support, and I’ll definitely think about reaching out again if I need to talk. Take care!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m really glad to hear that, Alex. Taking care of yourself is so important, and I’m here whenever you need support. Wishing you all the best as you prepare for the wedding. Take care, and remember to be kind to yourself!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you, I really appreciate that. I’ll keep working on being kind to myself. It’s nice to know I have someone to talk to if I need it. I’ll take care, and I hope things go well. Thanks again for everything!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Alex. It’s been a pleasure talking with you, and I’m glad you feel you have support. Remember, being kind to yourself is a journey, and you’re already taking important steps. Wishing you all the best, and take care!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thanks so much! It’s been good to talk things through. I’ll keep working on being kind to myself and remember the steps we discussed. I appreciate your support and encouragement. Take care, and I hope to chat again sometime!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m really glad to hear that, Alex. It’s been great to talk things through with you, and I’m here whenever you want to chat again. Take good care of yourself, and I hope everything goes well for you. Looking forward to our next conversation!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I really appreciate it. It’s been helpful to have this conversation, and I’ll definitely keep your words in mind. I’ll take care of myself, and I look forward to chatting again when I need to. Take care, and see you next time!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Alex! I’m glad our conversation has been helpful for you. Remember to take care of yourself, and I’ll be here whenever you want to chat again. Wishing you all the best until then. Take care, and see you next time!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thanks again! I really appreciate your support. I’ll take care and remember to reach out if I need to talk. Wishing you all the best too. See you next time!

transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Alex! I appreciate your kind words. Take care of yourself, and I’ll be here whenever you need to talk. Wishing you all the best as well. Looking forward to our next conversation! See you next time!
